description: Figure 9 presents the hydrogen embrittlement response of a low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel, specifically the kappa-carbide-strengthened Fe-26Mn-11Al-1.2C grade. The main panel shows engineering stress versus engineering plastic strain curves for specimens tested without hydrogen charging (black curves) and with hydrogen charging (red curves) under a protocol of 40 min precharging plus continuous in situ charging during tensile testing. The uncharged material sustains stresses near 1000 MPa and exhibits large ductility exceeding 40% plastic strain, whereas the hydrogen-charged specimens fracture prematurely at only ~2–5% plastic strain, revealing severe hydrogen-induced loss of ductility. The inset displays a thermal desorption analysis spectrum measured at a heating rate of 26 K/min, showing hydrogen desorption rate (wt ppm/s) versus temperature with the experimental curve, peak fit, and cumulative fit. The spectrum exhibits multiple desorption peaks; as the caption reports, these are associated with hydrogen trapping at kappa-carbide precipitates, with an estimated activation energy of ~76–80 kJ/mol from Redlich-Kister analysis. Together, the panels establish that kappa-carbides serve as hydrogen trap sites and that their presence markedly increases susceptibility to hydrogen embrittlement.
